headline: Lab-Grown Cells Give Blind Mice Their Vision Back
meta_description: Duke University researchers use stem cells to restore retinal function in mice, offering hope for millions with vision loss diseases.
summary_short: Biomedical engineers at Duke University have successfully used lab-grown stem cells to restore vision in mice with retinal disease.
body:
The retina, technically part of our central nervous system, is remarkably difficult to treat. Its specialized blood barrier protects the eye from harmful agents but also keeps most medicines out. When this barrier breaks down, vision loss follows—affecting millions worldwide.

Now, researchers at Duke University in North Carolina have found a way around this problem. Using induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs)—the technology that won the Nobel Prize in 2012—the team grew retinal blood vessel cells from scratch in the laboratory.

When these lab-grown cells were injected into mice with retinal disease, something remarkable happened: the cells integrated into the damaged tissue, regenerated blood vessels, and restored retinal function. The breakthrough proved especially promising for diabetic retinopathy, the leading cause of vision loss in working-age Americans.

What makes this different from current approaches? Today's retinal endothelial cells come from real patients, making them expensive and scarce. Growing them from stem cells could change everything.

"Being able to grow a continuous supply from scratch could offer many advantages," explained Parker Esswein, a PhD student on the research team published in Nature Biomedical Engineering.

The team plans to explore therapeutic applications through industry partnerships, with a patent already pending. For the millions living with retinal disease, the path forward just became clearer.
